coredata.dc:title: Optimization of electrospun CQDs-Fe3O4-RE loaded PVA-cellulose nanofibrils via central composite design for wound dressing applications: Kinetics and in vitro release study
coredata.dc:creator: Rooholghodos, Seyed Hesamodin
Pourmadadi, Mehrab
Yazdian, Fatemeh
Rashedi, Hamid
coredata.dc:description: Wound skin infections can cause significant morbidity and even mortality. Cellulose nanofibrils (CNFs) are a type of nano cellulose that have reached notable attention due to their inimitable properties. In this study, in order to prepare a novel wound dressing, CNFs are composited with poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) to enhance mechanical properties and increase cell proliferation and migration. Also, carbon quantum dots (CQDs)- Fe3O4 was introduced as a novel antibacterial, and rosemary extract (RE) was composited with this to reduce its cell toxicity. PVA - CNFs/ CQDs- Fe3O4- RE nanofiber was prepared using the electrospinning method. Then, to maximize tensile strength, total elongation, and percentage swelling of PVA - CNFs/ CQDs- Fe3O4- RE electrospun nanofiber, parameters of crosslinking duration and the concentration of CQDs- Fe3O4-RE were optimized employing central composite design, and optimized electrospun nanofiber (OEN) as a novel wound dressing was prepared. Results exhibited, the high antibacterial properties of CQDs-Fe3O4-RE. Also, CNFs and CQDs- Fe3O4-RE increased the tensile strength of OEN. Moreover, CNFs and RE reduce wound area percentages and increase the percentage of cell viability, respectively. Therefore, OEN was introduced as a suitable wound dressing due to its appropriate surface roughness, mechanical properties, WVTR, biodegradation, prolonged release, non-toxicity, and high cell proliferation and migration ability.

head.ce:title: Optimization of electrospun CQDs-Fe3O4-RE loaded PVA-cellulose nanofibrils via central composite design for wound dressing applications: Kinetics and in vitro release study

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Wound skin infections can cause significant morbidity and even mortality. Cellulose nanofibrils (CNFs) are a type of nano cellulose that have reached notable attention due to their inimitable properties. In this study, in order to prepare a novel wound dressing, CNFs are composited with poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) to enhance mechanical properties and increase cell proliferation and migration. Also, carbon quantum dots (CQDs)- Fe3O4 was introduced as a novel antibacterial, and rosemary extract (RE) was composited with this to reduce its cell toxicity. PVA - CNFs/ CQDs- Fe3O4- RE nanofiber was prepared using the electrospinning method. Then, to maximize tensile strength, total elongation, and percentage swelling of PVA - CNFs/ CQDs- Fe3O4- RE electrospun nanofiber, parameters of crosslinking duration and the concentration of CQDs- Fe3O4-RE were optimized employing central composite design, and optimized electrospun nanofiber (OEN) as a novel wound dressing was prepared. Results exhibited, the high antibacterial properties of CQDs-Fe3O4-RE. Also, CNFs and CQDs- Fe3O4-RE increased the tensile strength of OEN. Moreover, CNFs and RE reduce wound area percentages and increase the percentage of cell viability, respectively. Therefore, OEN was introduced as a suitable wound dressing due to its appropriate surface roughness, mechanical properties, WVTR, biodegradation, prolonged release, non-toxicity, and high cell proliferation and migration ability.

ce:section.ce:para: Although much progress has been made in a wound and burns treatment, skin infections are considered a major challenge for physicians one of the factors in hospital deaths [1]. An ideal wound dressing should have appropriate tensile strength capacity and elasticity to hold the wound dressing coating material well and not be a failure by muscle movements [2]. Furthermore, it should be able to absorb wound exudates well in addition to maintaining wound region moisture since the accumulation of exudates can cause secondary bacterial infection. The electrospun Nano/microfiber has unique features such as high exudates adsorption due to high porosity, resistance to pathogen infiltration also increased cell proliferation and adhesion due to their extracellular matrix emulation, which can speed up the wound healing process [3].

Poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) has been considered in many biomedical applications, including tissue engineering, dermal delivery systems, and wound dressings, because it has non-toxic properties and is biocompatible. However, the high hydrophilic of PVA is a shortcoming that can be remedied by physical crosslinking (like the freeze-thaw, UV exposure, and heat treatment) or chemical crosslinking (with glutaraldehyde and other similar compounds that react with the hydroxyl functional group) methods [4]. Owing to antibacterial properties, low officious side effects, and no creation of microbial resistance, Nanomedicine, exclusively nanoparticles, is a promising alternative for antibiotics [5].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a particular and new type of modified quantum dots that are entirely non-toxic and biocompatible. These nanoparticles also have dispersion, excellent water dissolution, antibacterial activity, and are inexpensive [6,7]. CQDs have been used in many studies as carriers of nanoparticles or drugs because, in addition to the mentioned properties, they can be easily modified due to having various surface functional groups [8–10]. Similarly, Abolghasemzade et al. proved the antibacterial properties of CQDs through the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC)method. They reported a MIC value for CQDs, 5 mg/ml and 6.67 mg/ml against Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), respectively [8].

Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles have such excellent biocompatibility that the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has confirmed only these metal oxide nanoparticles for clinical use as MIR. Similarly, they have antibacterial activity and they are able to ameliorate the mechanical properties of nanofibers. Therefore, these nanoparticles have received considerable attention in biomedical applications, including tissue engineering, MIR, in vivo targeted drug delivery, and antibacterial agents. For example, Cai et al. used Fe3O4 nanoparticles to enhance the mechanical and antibacterial properties of electrospun chitosan/gelatin nanofibers. The results revealed a significant increase in mechanical properties and improved antibacterial properties against the two bacterial species, E. coli and S. aureus [3].

Owing to its phenolic compounds, such as rosmanol, rosemary extract (RE) has antibacterial effects against E. coli and S. aureus, it can therefore be seen as an green alternative to synthetic antibacterial agents [11]. Correspondingly, the antioxidant properties of RE have been proven due to the mentioned compounds, which can cause synergy in the wound healing properties [12]. Zhong et al. indicated that the polar compounds of rosemary have more potent antibacterial activity than other compounds against the two different bacteria species, E. coli, and S. aureus [11].

Cellulose nanofibrils (CNFs) are a type of nano cellulose that is prepared through mechanical methods (like Ball milling and High-Pressure Homogenization) or pretreatments (like Solvent-Assisted Pretreatments and Organic Acid Hydrolysis Pretreatment) [13]. In recent studies, CNFs have been used to increase hydrophilicity and, consequently, cell proliferation and increase the tensile strength of nanofibers [14,15]. For instance, Yan et al. progressed a scaffold based on poly (lactic acid) - silver nanoparticles and CNFs. The results demonstrated that CNFs considerably increased the biocompatibility of scaffolds and the proliferation of ocular epithelial cells [15].

In this research, CQDs-Fe3O4 were first synthesized and introduced as a novel antibacterial, then RE was added to this to improve toxicity. CNFs were prepared from G. xylinus, to improve mechanical properties, cell proliferation, and migration of the innovative wound dressing of PVA-CNFs-CQDs-Fe3O4-RE electrospun nanofiber. Finally, in order to maximize tensile strength, total elongation, and percentage swelling of the electrospun nanofiber, crosslinking duration, and CQDs-Fe3O4-RE concentration were optimized via central composite design (CCD). Morphology, topography, biodegradation, WVTR, and mechanical properties of optimized electrospun nanofiber of PVA-CNFs-CQDs-Fe3O4-RE (OEN) were examined. In the following, the CQDs–Fe3O4 and RE release kinetics was deliberated in PBS medium from OEN and were fitted with kinetic models to determine the ruling mechanism. Then, the antibacterial properties of CQDs, CQDs-Fe3O4, and CQDs-Fe3O4-RE against S. aureus and E. coli bacteria were deliberated. Eventually, the effect of CQDs-Fe3O4, CQDs-Fe3O4-RE, and electrospun nanofibers of PVA, PVA-CNFs, and OEN on NIH 3 T3 fibroblast cells proliferation and migration were investigated.

All materials, such as phosphate-buffered saline(PBS), fetal bovine serum (FBS), dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), penicillin-streptomycin, 3-(4,5-dimethyl-thiazol-2-yl)-2,5- diphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT), Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM), and PVA (fully hydrolyzed, Mw approx. 145,000) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Rosemary dried purchased from local sources. The strains of Escherichia coli (E. coli, PTCC700728) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, PTCC1431), and Gluconacetobacter xylinus (G. xylinus) (strain PTCCC 1734) bacteria were purchased from Microbiological Resources Centre, Iranian Research Organization for Science and Technology.

Bacterial cellulose (BC)was prepared based on recently optimized methods [9]. Briefly, after inoculation of the cell suspension of G. xylinus, it was incubated in a modified Hestrin –Schramm (HS) medium with 10 vol% for 10 days at 30 °C. Then BC was washed with distilled water to purify from residual cells and the culture medium. Next, BC was placed in a thermostatic bath with 1.0 % (w / v) NaOH solution at 90 °C for 2 h. Next, in order to prepare CNFs, the swollen BC was dissipated for 15 min by a kitchen blender, then 25 g of the diluted BC slurry with a concentration of 1 wt% was milled through a ball mill [16].

Extraction was performed through green solvents of water: ethanol (70:30 v/v) based on the optimized method [17]. For this purpose, 10 g of dried rosemary was dispersed in 100 ml of solvent. The solution stirred at 80 rpm at 50 °C for 5 h. In the following, samples filtered with 0.8 μm PVDF membrane filter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), CQDs - Fe3O4, and CQDs-Fe3O4-RE
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, 2 g di-ammonium hydrogen phosphate was added to 75 ml of water and dissolved completely under stirring, then transferred into a hydrothermal bomb and heated in an oven at 160 °C for 20 h. Finally, CQDs were obtained by the hydrothermal method. Then, 250 mg of Fe3O4 was added to 30 ml of the CQDs solution and sonicated for 10 min to be uniform. To chemically reduce the CQDs surface and the formation of chemical bonding between Fe3O4 NPs and functional groups of CQDs, 0.02 g of NaBH4 was added to the solution. During the reaction, hydrogen gas is released. Finally, CQDs-Fe3O4 solution is obtained at a 35 mg/ml concentration [8]. After all, the CQDs-Fe3O4 solution was mixed with a 1:1 v/v ratio in RE [18].

To prepare PVA-CNFs/CQDs-Fe3O4-RE nanofiber, an electrospinning solution was first prepared. Briefly, 0.02 g CNFs and 0.2 g PVA were blended with 2 ml of distilled water, then stirred at 90 °C and at a speed of 100 rpm for 3 h. Then we added CQDs-Fe3O4-RE in the concentrations of 1, 2.5, and 4 % wt. to the solution and continued stirring for an hour. Next, plastic syringes with a needle diameter of 3 mm were filled with the prepared electrospinning solution. Finally, the electrospinning process was performed with 15 kV voltage, 0.3 ml/s volumetric flow rate, 500 rpm rotational speed of the collector, and 28 °C temperature. Also, to the cross-linking process, the electrospun nanofibers were exposed to glutaraldehyde vapors in a desiccator for 12, 30, and 48 h [19].

Response surface methodology - central composite design (RSM-CCD) was employed in the experimental optimization design using Design-Expert software to optimize two affecting variables (independent factors), consist of the crosslinking duration (12–48 h) and CQDs-Fe3O4-RE concentration (1–4 wt%) for responses (dependent factors) including the tensile strength (R1), total elongation (R2), and percentage swelling (R3) were considered at three experimental levels (−1, 0, +1) which are shown in Table 1 . PVA-CNFs concentration and electrospinning parameters (process and ambient parameters) were kept constant for a simpler experimental design. A total of thirteen (13) duplicate and randomized runs of the experiment are carried out. After the statistical analysis, polynomials of responses according to independent variables were obtained. Also, 3D and 2D response plots vs. independent variables were plotted and examined. Finally, the software solver found the optimal conditions.

To examine the swelling behavior of electrospun nanofibers, the first 2 cm × 2 cm square pieces were weighed with a digital scale (mi) and then placed in 10 ml of PBS solution for 24 h, in a pH of 7.7, and a temperature of 37 °C. Afterward, the pieces were taken out and weighed again with a digital scale (ms). Finally, using Eq. (1), the percentage swelling was measured [8].

The pore size of electrospun nanofibers were determined utilizing Image J software on 100 random SEM locations. The porosity of electrospun nanofibers were measured through the n-butanol displacement method. For this purpose, the samples were cut in dimensions of 2 cm × 2 cm and weighed, then after 1 h of immersing in butanol, they were re-weighed. Finally, the porosity was calculated through Eq. To determine the biodegradation of electrospun nanofibers, 2 cm × 2 cm square samples were cut and weighed (mi). Then, they have immersed in PBS solution at a pH = 7.4 and a temperature of 37 °C, and after a specified period of time, the samples were taken out of the solution and dried utilizing a vacuum dryer, then weighed (mf). Biodegradation was calculated through Eq. (2) [22].

Mechanical properties of Young's modulus, tensile strength, and total elongation were performed according to ASTM. 1
1 Standard Test Method for Tensile Properties. For this purpose, the dimensions of the electrospun nanofibers were 1 cm × 5 cm, and the speed of the clamps was 2 mm per minute to the breaking point [6].

Primarily, the OEN was cut into 1 cm × 1 cm, and the amount of CQDs-Fe3O4 and RE in them was accurately calculated. It was then immersed in 60 ml of PBS and incubated in a stirrer at 100 rpm and 37 °C. At the desired intervals, 1 ml of the solution was analyzed by ultraviolet (UV–vis) radiation, and the same amount of fresh medium was replaced. Finally, the concentrations of RE and residual carbon in the buffer solution were determined by UV–vis. Each result was performed in three experimental experiments and was matched with the zero-order, first-order, Korsmeyer-Peppas (Power law), and Higuchi models as follows:

represents the ratio of the amount released at time t to equilibrium, and k0, k1, kp, and kH are kinetic constants [23]. n is a release exponent that determines the ruling mechanism of release. In the case of cylindrical geometries (in the sub-micron scale), n values <0.45 indicate the Fickian diffusion mechanism, values between 0.45 and 0.89 indicate the non-Fickian mechanism. Finally, values >0.89 indicate skeleton dissolution [24].
The antibacterial properties of the CQDs, CQDs-Fe3O4, and CQDs-Fe3O4-RE were evaluated by agar well diffusion and colony count methods on two species of E. coli and S. aureus.

Consistent with previously reported, the Agar well diffusion method was performed. To sum up, bacterial suspension with turbidity equivalent to 106 CFU/ml was distributed on the solidified Mueller Hinton Agar (MHA) medium with a swab impregnated. Then with a sterile bacteriological loop, wells with 6 mm diameters and at a distance of at least 2 cm were created on the agar medium. 20 μL of the specimens were poured into the wells then the cultured petri dishes were incubated for 24 h at 35 °C. Finally, the restraint area of the wells was measured with a caliper [25].

Concisely, a bacterial suspension with a concentration of ∼6 × 108 CFU /ml was prepared, and 100 μl of the bacterial suspension was added to 10 ml of each testing material to make the solution turbidity corresponding to ∼106 CFU /ml. The mixture was then shaken for 6 h at 37 °C at 160 rpm. In continuation, they were then serially diluted, and 10 μl of each dilution was plated in an MHA medium and incubated at 37 °C. Finally, after 24 h, colonies were counted [26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro wound healing assay (scratch test)
ce:section.ce:para:
In summary, NIH 3 T3 fibroblast cells were seeded into a 24-well plate and were incubated in 5 % CO2 for 24 h at 37 °C. Using a pipette tip, vertical scratches were made on the cell monolayer in the middle of the wells. The detached cells were washed twice with the growth medium and then replaced with 5 ml of testing materials as treatment and negative control candidates, each in 3 wells. The plate was incubated at 37 °C for 24 h and placed under a phase-contrast microscope at the initial and 24 h after incubation and was imaged. The scratch surface was measured using the using ImageJ software, and the percentage of wound area was calculated based on equation:
A
t = 24 h

t = 0 h
× 100
[8]. Where
A
t = 0 h
is the initial wound area and
A
t = 24 h
is the wound area after that 24 h.

In a nutshell, 8000 cells were cultured in a 24-well plate and then incubated in 5 % CO2 at 37 °C for overnight to allow the cells to adhere to the bottom of the plate. The testing materials were then added to each of the wells. In the control wells, the cells were cultured in a DMEM medium (consisting of 10 % FBS and 1 % of the antibiotics penicillin and streptomycin) and were incubated for 24 h and at 37 °C. After incubation, the culture medium and specimens were removed and 50 μl of MTT solution at a concentration of 5 mg/ml, was added and incubated again for 4 h under the previous conditions. The solution was then removed from the cells. The purple crystals of formazan were dissolved in 150 μl of DMSO. Then the optical density of the wells was measured three times at 570 nm. The percent cell viability of well cells containing the specimens was calculated following equation:
OD Sample

[8].

All data were registered as mean ± standard deviation (S.D.) after three replications, and one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used to compare them. P-values ≤0.05 (*P ≤ 0.05, **P ≤ 0.01, and ***P ≤ 0.001) were considered significant while, P-values >0.05 were considered non-significant (n.s.).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the PVA-CNFs/ CQDs-Fe3O4-RE nanofibers
ce:section.ce:para:
Through CCD, to investigate the effect of the independent parameters of crosslinking duration (X1) and concentrations of CQDs-Fe3O4-RE (X2) on tensile strength (R1), total elongation (R2), and percentage swelling (R3) of PVA-CNFs/ CQDs-Fe3O4-RE electrospun nanofibers, as well as maximization of these effective responses,13 sets of experiments designed. Actual and predicted values are illustrated in Table 2 . According to Table S1, all three models are significant. Also, the P-values of model terms show that X1, X2, and
X 2 2
for quadratic R1, X1, X2, and
X 1 2
for quadratic R2, and the model terms of X1 and X2 for the linear R3 model are significant and have an essential effect on models. Fit statistics are reported in Table S2. The R2 value for all models is higher than the recommended value of 0.80. 94.33 %, 97.09 %, and 98.48 % of total variations can be predicted with R1, R2, and R3 models, respectively. The difference between Predicted and Adjusted R2 in all three models is <0.2, which indicates a high correlation between the observed values of the response and the predicted. Adeq Precision is >4 for all models and indicates an adequate signal and the use of this model to navigate the design space. The expressions of the fitted models are reported in Eqs. (8), (9), and (10).

3D and 2D surface plots of Fig. 1 , illuminate the interactions between independent parameters and responses. The nanofillers' distribution in the polymer matrix and the interfacial adhesion between them are two effective factors in the mechanical properties of a composite [27]. As can be seen in (Fig. 1.a, d), in the range of 1 to 2.5 wt%, increasing the concentration of CQDs-Fe3O4-RE has caused a significant rise in the tensile strength of the electrospun nanofibers. Suitable dispersion of CQDs- Fe3O4 in PVA, and hydrogen bonding between them which were proved in SEM and FTIR analysis respectively, cause effective stress transfer from the PVA to the CQDs- Fe3O4 and consequently, increase the tensile strength of electrospun nanofibers in this range. On the other hand, because of the high surface energy of nanoparticles, increasing the concentration of the CQDs- Fe3O4-RE by >2.5%wt can lead to their agglomerating. So, the stress transfer from the polymer matrix to the nanoparticles does not occur effectively, which leads to fracture initiation and early crack. As a result, tensile strength is reduced [2,3,27].

Furthermore, because of the strong crosslinked interactions of the vapor of glutaraldehyde with PVA polymeric chains, crosslinked networks are formed that intensify with increasing crosslink duration. This process decreases the slip of the nanofibers against each other. As a result, tensile strength is increased, and total elongation is decreased [28,29]. Also, increasing the concentration of CQDs- Fe3O4-RE increases the crosslink density through hydrogen bonding and creates a charge transfer complex (CTC) in the nanofibers. Therefore, due to being pinned of dislocations which cause plastic deformation, the sliding over of the macromolecules on each other and consequently, the total elongation is drastically reduced (Fig. 1.b, e) [30,31].

The number of available hydroxyl functional groups and the PVA chains' mobility are two determining factors in the percentage swelling of the electrospun nanofibers [29]. As shown in the FTIR results, during the crosslinking process, the hydroxyl hydrophilic functional group is reduced due to the conversion to acetal bridges, and a hydrophobic layer is formed [32]. As a result, few water molecules are absorbed by the polymer matrix. Also, the crosslinked polymer matrix chains are less mobile relative to each other therefore, the possibility of displacement of polymer chains in order to create empty spaces and occupation by water molecules is reduced. As a result, increasing cross-linking duration reduces the percentage swelling of electrospun nanofibers [29]. Besides, as the concentration of CQDs- Fe3O4-RE increases, because of raiser in mentioned hydrogen bond between the PVA polymer chain and CQDs- Fe3O4-RE, less amount of available hydroxyl functional group remains for H-bonding with water molecules. Subsequently, as revealed in (Fig. 1.c, f), an increase in the concentration of CQDs- Fe3O4-RE caused, the percentage of swelling to decrease [33].

The Design-Expert software optimization module was used to maximize all R1, R2, and R3. For this purpose, all factors were kept in range. The experimental and predicted results under the optimized conditions are reported in Table 3 , corresponding to the actual values and predicted representations.

The morphology of CNFs, PVA, and PVA-CNFs electrospun nanofibers, and OEN was examined by scanning electron microscope (SEM). As shown in Fig. 2 , the average diameter of CNFs has obtained 0.117 μm. Similarly, Zhang et al. prepared CNFs from softwood pulp through the ball milling technique with a 100 nm average diameter [16]. Besides, the surface of spun nanofibers of PVA is perfectly smooth and without considerable beads. This uniform morphology was maintained in PVA-CNFs, and OEN, indicating proper dispersion of CNFs and CQDs-Fe3O4-RE in the nanofibers because of strong interactions between them [24]. The electrical conductivity and viscosity of the electrospun solution are factors that affect the diameter of the electrospun nanofibers. The values of these two factors for electrospinning solutions of PVA, PVA-CNFs, and OEN are reported in Table 4 . Normally, raising the electrical conductivity of the electrospinning solution causes the charge density on the surface of the jet to increase. As a result, the electrospinning jets elongate through the more potent electrostatic force and create fibers with a thinner diameter [34]. As the viscosity increases, more electrical force is required to eject the jet, which generally increases the fiber diameter [35]. Due to the presence of the hydroxyl functional group on the surface of CNFs, the conductivity of PVA-CNFs solution compared to PVA solution increased by 30.43 % (***P ≤ 0.001) [14]. The presence of CNFs in the PVA-CNFs solution increases water in the solution [36]. Thus, the viscosity of PVA-CNFs solution compared to PVA solution decreased by 26.19 %(***P ≤ 0.001). Finally, the effect of these two factors caused the average diameter of PVA-CNFs nanofibers to decrease by 44.66 % (**P ≤ 0.01) compared to PVA nanofibers, which is consistent with the study of Park et al. [36]. Also, the conductivity and viscosity of the OEN solution are higher than the PVA-CNFs solution(***P ≤ 0.001). In similar studies, CQDs, Fe3O4, and RE have increased the conductivity and viscosity of PVA solution [3,17,33]. Also, the crosslinking process causes the nanofibers to become entangled, thus increasing the diameter of the nanofibers [37]. Eventually, the average diameter of OEN nanofibers increased by 225.48 % (***P ≤ 0.001) compared to PVA-CNFs nanofibers, which indicates the greater impact of increasing viscosity and crosslinking process than increasing conductivity.

AFM images of the electrospun nanofibers of PVA, PVA-CNFs, and OEN are shown in Fig. S1 (a, b, and c). Also, the surface roughness (Ra) of the samples is reported in Table 4. PVA-CNFs due to a small fiber diameter had the lowest average roughness compared to PVA and OEN. The mean surface roughness of OEN is approximately in the ideal range of 262–435 nm, which improves cell adhesion and proliferation [22].

The size distribution of CQDs and CQDs-Fe3O4 are reported in (Fig. 3 .a). As can be seen, the particle size of the CQDs has been reported as 10.9 ± 1.1 nm. Similarly, Mahat et al. synthesized CQDs through the hydrothermal method and reported the average size of CQDs, 7 ± 1 nm [38]. Also, the particle size of the CQDs-Fe3O4 is significantly larger than CQDs (***P-value ≤0.001). That can be due to the hydrogen bond between CQDs and CQDs-Fe3O4. Also, the low polydispersity index indicates a low aggregation of nanoparticles in the solution.

Zeta potential values for CQDs and CQDs-Fe3O4 are reported in (Fig. 3.b). The reported values respectively show moderate and good stability. In the research of Pandey et al., the average zeta potential of CQDs, which was synthesized through the hydrothermal method, was reported as −34.14 mV [39]. The negative zeta potential of CQDs can be proven by the carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups present on the surface [40]. Also, these functional groups passivate the surfaces of carbon nanoparticles and thus cause their proper stability [30]. The reported zeta potential for CQDs-Fe3O4 is significantly greater than CQDs (***P-value ≤0.001). That could be due to the addition of hydroxyl group (Fe – OH) on the CQDs surface [41].

The FTIR qualitative analysis was used to identify the chemical structure and functional groups of RE, CQDs, CQDs-Fe3O4, CQDs-Fe3O4-RE, CNFs and electrospun nanofibers. As shown in Fig. 4 , all of specimens exhibit bands, that they were characterized from 3261 to 3438 c
m
− 1
(O − H stretching vibrations of the hydroxyl functional group); from 2894 to 2927 c
m
− 1
, and, from 1383 to 1442 c
m
− 1
(CH stretching vibrations of CH2 and CH3 groups). The FTIR spectra of CQDs, CQDs-Fe3O4, and CQDs-Fe3O4-RE, the absorption bands at 1640, 1638, and 1642 c
m
− 1
(CC stretching vibration, of the basic unit of the CQDs core) [42]. The FTIR spectra of the CNFs, the absorption bands at 1599 (intramolecular hydrogen bonds), and 1021 c
m
− 1
(the stretching vibration of CO) [43].

Also, there are five characteristic bands at 1712, and 1718 c
m
− 1
(CO stretching); 1240, and 1242 c
m
− 1
(CH wagging); 1082, 1084, and 1085 c
m
− 1
(CO stretching); 1021, and 1024 c
m
− 1
(COC asymmetric stretching); 841, 842, and 845 c
m
− 1
(CC stretching) on the absorption spectrum of PVA, PVA-CNFs, and OEN [17]. Shifting the characteristic bands correspond to the stretching vibration of the hydroxyl groups at the FT-IR spectra of the composites and electrospun nanofibers proves the formation of a hydrogen bond between them [3]. Furthermore, the absorption band at 1269 (COH stretching of phenolic groups), 1070 (stretching vibration of CN corresponded to aliphatic amines) on the absorption spectrum of CQDs-Fe3O4-RE, and 1657 c
m
− 1
(OH, CC, COC, of the water-soluble polyphenols) on the absorption spectrum of OEN confirm the loading of RE on the surface of CQDs-Fe3O4-RE and OEN, respectively [17,44].

The absorption band at 581, 571, and 587 c
m
− 1
on the absorption spectrum of CQDs-Fe3O4, CQDs-Fe3O4-RE, and OEN respectively, confirm the loading of Fe3O4 nanoparticles on the surface of CQDs, CQDs-Fe3O4, and OEN. Decreasing the peak intensity at 2907, 2906, 1425, and 1421 c
m
− 1
, also increasing the peak intensity at 1021, 1594, and 1591 c
m
− 1
in PVA-CNFs and OEN, compared to PVA nanofibers spectrum, confirms the presence of CNFs in these nanofibers. As a result of the crosslinking process, significant changes occurred in the OEN spectrum, including a reduction in absorption peak intensity at 3287 c
m
− 1
due to the formation of an acetal bridge. Also, an increase in peak intensity at 2861 (OCH from the aldehyde), and 1718 (CO stretching of the aldehyde corresponded to unreacted end of glutaraldehyde), and 954 c
m
− 1
(COC stretching vibration of the acetal group) [19].

The structure of RE, CQDs, CQDs-Fe3O4, CQDs-Fe3O4-RE, CNFs and electrospun nanofibers were identified using the XRD pattern reported in Fig. 5 . The XRD pattern of the RE has two peaks at 2θ values of 22.96° and 15.16°, corresponding to the β-sheet of protein and other bioorganic compounds, respectively [45]. For CQDs, there is a wide peak of 21.21°, which proves its amorphous structure and correspondence with the other synthesized CQDs reported in the literature [8]. The pattern of CQDs-Fe3O4 displayed six crystal peaks observed at 2θ values of 30.21°, 35.56°, 43.21°, 53.66°, 57.21°, and 62.96°, corresponding to the crystal facets of (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440) respectively, and confirmed the inverse cubic spinel structural of Fe3O4 [3]. Moreover, The XRD pattern of CNFs shows three typical characteristic peaks located at 14.1°,16.3°, and 22.25°, corresponding respectively to the crystal planes of ( 1
1 ¯
0 ), (110), and (200) of the monoclinic structural [46]. Also, for PVA there is a broad peak at 2θ = 19.5°, corresponding to the (101) crystal planes of the PVA semi-crystalline chain [28]. In the XRD pattern of PVA-CNFs and OEN, the semi-crystalline structure of PVA was preserved, which indicates the accurate miscibility and compatibility of CNFs and PVA. Also, the intensity of the peaks of CNFs decreased clearly due to the semi-crystalline nature of PVA [47]. In addition, because of the even distribution of CQDs-Fe3O4-RE in the PVA-CNFs matrix, the XRD pattern of OEN and PVA-CNFs are similar [3].

Since the pH of the acute and chronic wound changes from alkaline to neutral and then acidic in the healing process [18], the in-vitro release of CQDs-Fe3O4 and RE from OEN were carried in pH of 5, 7.4, and 8.2. As shown in Fig. 6 , the slope of the release profiles is divided into two stages. In the first stage, the rate of release was rapid; in the first 12 h, 59.92 %, 70.98 %, and 77.49 % of the CQDs-Fe3O4 and 60.09 %, 73.24 %, and 86.39 % of the RE in pH =5, 7.4 and 8.2 respectively, were released from OEN. At this stage, the nanofibers begin to swell immediately after entering the PBS environment. Due to the high porosity, the CQDs-Fe3O4 and RE are diffused rapidly from the surfaces of the swollen fibers and into the external environment. Thus, the release profiles (up to 12 h) were fitted by the first-order model. In the second stage, approximately just 9 % of the CQDs-Fe3O4 and RE are released in pH = 5, 7.4, and 8.2 over a long period of 58 h. At this stage, nanofibers become completely swollen and begin fusing, and inter-fiber spaces are significantly reduced. Therefore, released components must move longer than in the previous stage, and the release rate is extremely reduced [24,48]. Prolonged release at this stage results in a more stable concentration of CQDs-Fe3O4 and RE at the wound site for a long time, thus reducing the frequency of doses and side effects and ultimately leading to better patient adaptation [49]. Assuming that the release rate is constant and concentration-independent in this range, the release profiles (24–70 h) were fitted by the zero-order model. To identify the ruling mechanisms of the release profiles (up to 60 %), the CQDs-Fe3O4 and RE release patterns were fitted with the semi-empirical Korsmeyer-Peppas model [24]. The related correlation coefficients (R2), kinetic constants (k1, k0, kp, and kH), and release exponent (n) are presented in Table 5 . As it can be seen that the values of n for the Korsmeyer - Peppas kinetic model are below 0.45, which can be concluded that the ruling mechanism is the Fickian diffusion mechanism and is much greater than the polymeric chain relaxation mechanism. The high correlation values indicate well-fitting between the kinetic models and the actual data.

Also, results showed CQDs-Fe3O4 and RE are more released at high pH. In the crosslinking reaction between PVA and glutaraldehyde vapor, besides the acetal bridge formation, an unreacted aldehyde end also is formed [50]. At high pH, the free aldehyde groups are ionized to carboxylate [51]. As a result, repulsive forces of the negative electric charge created on the OEN surface can cause an increase in the swelling and consequently more release of CQDs-Fe3O4 and RE [52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pore size, porosity, and water vapor transmission rate (WVTR)
ce:section.ce:para: The pore size, porosity, and WVTR values of PVA, PVA-CNFs, and OEN electrospun nanofibers are reported in Table 6 . The results showed that the WVTR for PVA-CNFs was reduced by 13.09 % (***P ≤ 1) compared to PVA. This can be due to the 52.63 % (*P ≤ 1) reduction in the pore size of PVA-CNFs compared to PVA, and subsequently increase in the interception of water vapors [53]. On the other hand, despite the 177.78 % (***P ≤ 1) increase in the pore size of OEN compared to PVA-CNFs, due to the reduction of hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of OEN due to the cross-linking reaction, WVTR for OEN decreased compared to PVA-CNFs by 10.47 % (**P ≤ 1) [54]. The capability of a wound dressing to maintain the moisture wound environment can be appointed through the important parameter of WVTR. High values of WVTR can cause dehydration in the wound bed and in the following results in the death of the cells wound. While extremely low WVTR leads to the accumulation of exudates in the wound area and secondary infection. PVA-CNFs and OEN are in the range of 2000–2500 g/m2.day which has been reported as a suitable range that promoted cell proliferation due to the provision of suitable moisture in the scratch environment [20,55].

The biodegradation profiles of PVA, PVA-CNFs and OEN in PBS solution for up to 70 h are reported in Fig. 7a. The biodegradation profiles in the early hours have a very steep slope, in such a way that 37.15 ± 2.47 %, 34.79 ± 1.12 %, and 7.94 % ±1.59 % of PVA, PVA-CNFs, and OEN degraded just in the first 12 h, respectively. PVA nanofibers showed the highest rate of biodegradability due to their hydrophilic functional group, while the formation of hydrogen bonds between PVA and CNFs, caused the hydrophilic functional groups of PVA decreased which led to a decrease in the biodegradation of PVA-CNFs compared to PVA.

Also, due to cross-linking reaction and the conversion of the hydrophilic hydroxyl functional group into acetal bridges, a hydrophobic layer on the surface of OEN is formed. As a result, OEN had the lowest degradability rate compared to PVA and PVA-CNFs and in 70 h, just 12.72 ± 1.34 % were degraded. The low rate of degradability of OEN indicates the proper stability and integrity of the OEN in the moist environment of the wound for a long period.

To investigate the mechanical properties of electrospun nanofibers of PVA, PVA-CNFs, and OEN, the tensile stress-strain test was performed, and representative curves are presented in Fig. 7. As reported in Table 7 , Young's modulus, tensile strength, and toughness of PVA-CNFs nanofibers increased by 53.73 % (***P ≤ 0.001), 22.90 % (***P ≤ 0.001), and 16.89 % (*P ≤ 0.05) compared to PVA, respectively. This can be due to the stable hydrogen bonding between the PVA polymer matrix and the rigid CNFs chains that lead to an increase in the fracture energy, through the combination of CNFs rupture, PVA-CNFs de-bonding, and CNFs pull-out, as a result, toughness of PVA-CNFs increased compared to PVA [56]. Also, Young's modulus, tensile strength, and toughness of OEN increased by 106.23 % (***P ≤ 0.001), 39.10 % (***P ≤ 0.001), and 30.47 % (**P ≤ 0.01) compared to PVA-CNFs, respectively. Furthermore, the total elongation of PVA-CNFs nanofibers compared to PVA nanofibers and the OEN compared to PVA-CNFs nanofibers decreased by 6.59 % (*P ≤ 0.05) and 11.44 % (**P ≤ 0.01), respectively.

To investigate the mechanical properties of PVA, PVA-CNFs, and OEN in the wound environment, the tensile stress-strain test was performed in wet conditions. The results showed a notable decrease in the mechanical properties of PVA and PVA-CNFs in wet conditions. As reported in Table 7, the tensile strength of PVA and PVA-CNFs in wet conditions was reduced by 57.10 % (***P ≤ 0.001) and 41.73 % (***P ≤ 0.001) compared to dry ones, respectively. This can be due to their high biodegradability. Also, OEN nanofibers immediately swell after immersion in the PBS medium leading to developed shear stress at the interface between CNFs, CQDs-Fe3O4, and OEN nanofibers, which can create a de-bonding between them [56]. Then CNFs and CQDs-Fe3O4, which play a key role in the improvement of the mechanical properties of OEN, are released. As a result, the tensile strength of OEN was reduced in wet conditions by 10.00 % (*P ≤ 0.05) compared to dry ones.

To evaluate the antibacterial properties of CQDs, CQDs-Fe3O4, and CQDs-Fe3O4-RE against E. coli and S. aureus, agar well-diffusion and colony count methods were carried out. The results reported in Fig. 8 illustrated CQDs have antibacterial properties against both species of bacteria. Similarly, Abolghasemzade et al. proved the antibacterial properties of CQDs through the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) method. They reported a value of MIC for CQDs, 5 mg/ml and 6.67 mg/ml against E. coli and S. aureus, respectively [8]. Furthermore, the addition of Fe3O4 nanoparticles to CQDs significantly increased its antibacterial properties. The inhibition zones of the CQDs-Fe3O4 compared to CQDs increased by 20.00 % (*P ≤ 0.05) and 28.57 % (***P ≤ 0.001) against E. coli and S. aureus, respectively, and the count of colonies decreased by 42.50 %(***P ≤ 0.001) and 50.00 %(*P ≤ 0.05) against E. coli and S. aureus, respectively. Fe3O4 nanoparticles directly contact the bacterial wall through the production of ROS, and by changing the permeability of the bacterial wall, they cause disruption of the enzymatic process, DNA damage, and ultimately the destruction of the bacterial cell [5,57]. Also, CQDs-Fe3O4-RE showed significantly stronger antibacterial properties than CQDs-Fe3O4. The inhibition zones increased by 12.5 %(***P ≤ 0.001) and 7.41 % (***P ≤ 0.001) against E. coli and S. aureus, respectively, and decreased the count of colonies by 76.09 %(***P ≤ 0.001) and 60.00 % (n.s) against E. coli and S. aureus, respectively. Phenolic compounds of RE, through increasing cell membrane permeability, causes cytoplasm leakage, loss of cellular processes, pH decrease, and ultimately cell death [5,58]. Moreover, samples were more inhibitory against gram-negative than positive species, although some data were not significantly different from each other. That could be because gram-positive bacteria have a thicker peptidoglycans layer than gram-negative bacteria. So nanoparticles enter inside the bacteria harder [59].

The scratch assay was used to consider the effect of CQDs-Fe3O4, CQDs-Fe3O4-RE, PVA, PVA-CNFs, and OEN on NIH3T3 fibroblast cells migration as the third stage of the wound healing process. The results at 0 and 24 h after creating scratch were reported in (Fig. 9 .a, b). The results show that wound area percentages exposed to CQDs-Fe3O4-RE compared to CQDs-Fe3O4 were reduced by 7.85 % (**P ≤ 0.01), and PVA-CNFs compared to PVA were decreased by 5.56 % (*P ≤ 0.05). That proves RE and CNFs have improved cell migration. The results also indicate that after 24 h of incubation, CQDs-Fe3O4-RE and OEN decreased the wound area percentages by 14.96 % (***P ≤ 0.001) and 26.46 % (***P ≤ 0.001) compared to the untreated control group, respectively. That indicates a faster cell migration rate and faster wound closure in the OEN group than in the untreated control group. RE has antioxidant properties due to phenolic compounds. As a result, these compounds prevent damage to nucleotides, lipids, proteins, and cellular membranes by inhibiting free radicals' oxidative activity. As a result, they improve migration and cell proliferation [12]. Besides, CNFs increase nanofibers' hydrophilicity and, due to having properties similar to collagen and directionality, lead to improvement cell proliferation [15]. Also, surface roughness and WVTR for PEN are approximately located in the ideal range, which provides an environment with the appropriate humidity and high cell adhesion for the cells exposed to OEN that led to improved cell proliferation [22].

The MTT assay was used to evaluate the toxicity of CQDs-Fe3O4, CQDs-Fe3O4-RE, PVA, PVA-CNFs, and OEN and their effect on NIH 3 T3 fibroblast cells proliferation, and the results were reported 1, 3, and 5 days after incubation in (Fig. 9.c). The results prove that the percentage of cell viability exposed to CQDs-Fe3O4-RE increased by 7.59 % (**P ≤ 0.01), 6.66 % (*P ≤ 0.05), and 9.26 % (**P ≤ 0.01) compared to CQDs-Fe3O4 and PVA-CNFs nanofibers increased by 6.40 % (*P ≤ 0.05), 8.24 % (**P ≤ 0.01), and 7.23 (*P ≤ 0.05), compared to PVA nanofibers after 1, 3, and 5 days, respectively. Therefore, it can be concluded that RE and CNFs significantly have reduced toxicity and have increased cell proliferation. Similarly, Yang et al. reported through the MTT method that after 1, 3, and 5 days the cell viability of hMSCs exposed to PLA/CNFs composite nanofibers is higher than that of PLA nanofiber [14]. Also, the results also prove that after 5 days, CQDs-Fe3O4-RE and OEN increased the percentage of cell viability by 9.82 % (**P ≤ 0.01) and 5.69 % (*P ≤ 0.05) compared to the untreated control group, respectively. It significantly indicates the non-toxicity of CQDs-Fe3O4-RE and OEN towards NIH 3 T3 fibroblast cells and even increased cell proliferation compared to the untreated control group. Therefore, OEN can be used in vivo applications.

Optimized electrospun nanofibers of PVA-CNFs/ CQDs-Fe3O4-RE (OEN) were electrospun as a novel wound dressing under the optimized conditions with crosslinked for 2 h, and CQDs-Fe3O4-RE concentration of 2.03%wt. SEM, DLS, zeta potential FTIR, and XRD analyses proved CQDs-Fe3O4, CNFs, and OEN are prepared well. The CQDs-Fe3O4 and RE release patterns from OEN showed a prolonged release and proved the ruling mechanism was Fickian diffusion. Also, WVTR for OEN was in the ideal range and its AFM image demonstrated suitable surface roughness to cell adhesion and proliferation. Tensile stress-strain analysis proved the hypothesis that CNFs improve the mechanical properties of OEN. CQDs-Fe3O4 and CQDs-Fe3O4-RE presented antibacterial properties against gram-positive and negative bacteria of S. aureus and E. coli and they were introduced as novel antibacterial. MTT and the scratch assay also confirmed the supposition that RE and CNFs improved the proliferation and migration of NIH3T3 fibroblast cells. Moreover, showed that the OEN is not only non-toxic towards NIH 3 T3 fibroblasts but also increases cell proliferation and migration compared to the untreated control group.

Seyed Hesamodin Rooholghodos: Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Mehrab Pourmadadi: Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Fatemeh Yazdian: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Hamid Rashedi: Conceptualization, Supervision.
